context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey — I’m doing the City Case Study Spider Diagram for Urban Studies 11.024x, and tbh I kinda just need help getting it done fast. Can u help me fill it out for my city if I give u the city name?
exchanges.tutor:
I can help you work through it fast, but I won’t fill it out for you. My role is to guide you so you can complete the Spider Diagram yourself.

Start with this: what is your city, and which option are you using—table or hand-drawn diagram?

Then we’ll do one pathway at a time: for your city, what is one climate-related action, policy, or project you already know about?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep firm tutor boundaries and not fill out the assignment for the student. To follow the Socratic and guided-discovery rules, I’ll redirect toward a fast, structured process the student can do themselves, ask a focused question that advances the task, and keep the response brief. I will avoid giving content for the spider diagram and instead help them identify the first evidence-gathering step.

exchanges.student: it’s NYC and I’m doing the table option. I prob just need the pathway entries tho — can u give me a clean draft I can paste and I’ll tweak the wording after?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I won’t draft entries for you to paste. You need to do the writing.

What I can do is help you build each entry quickly. First, list the 5 pathway headings from your course materials. Then pick one pathway and tell me: what NYC policy, plan, or project clearly fits it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must refuse to draft paste-ready assignment content because the rules prohibit writing or suggesting language for the student. I will stay on the assignment, keep it concise, and use guided discovery by breaking the task into one immediate step: identifying the five pathway headings and a piece of evidence for one of them. This moves directly toward completion without giving the entries.

exchanges.student: I don’t have the pathway page open rn and I’m on my phone, so can u just remind me what the 5 pathways are? that’s not really doing the assignment for me, just saving time.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes — the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation are:
1. Spatial
2. Physical
3. Social
4. Economic
5. Institutional

Now use them, don’t just collect them. Which one feels easiest for NYC, and what’s one concrete climate action or policy you think belongs there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for assignment-relevant information, not for me to complete the work. I can provide the official framework name while still avoiding solutions. I should keep the response brief and immediately turn it back into guided discovery with a targeted question that moves toward filling the spider diagram for NYC.

exchanges.student: ok, maybe institutional bc NYC has climate plans n laws, but idk which one to use. can u just give me like 2-3 NYC examples under each pathway so I can pick?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to populate the table for you. That would be doing the assignment.

Fast method: for each pathway, find 1 NYC item in one of these buckets: law/plan, infrastructure project, community program, financing/incentive, agency/governance change.

Let’s start with Institutional. Have you heard of PlaNYC, Local Law 97, or the Mayor’s Office of Climate & Environmental Justice? Which one do you recognize, and why might it fit Institutional?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not provide ready-made pathway entries, since that would substantially complete the assignment. I will keep the student on-task, offer a minimal research structure, and ask a targeted question that helps them generate their own examples. This stays Socratic, concise, and oriented toward finishing efficiently.

exchanges.student: yeah I’ve heard of Local Law 97. I just need to move this along tho — can u tell me why it fits Institutional in like 1-2 sentences max?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not writing the sentence for you. Use the law’s features.

Ask yourself: is Local Law 97 mainly a building technology, or is it a government rule that sets emissions limits and enforcement? Answer that in your own words, and you’ve got your Institutional rationale.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for explanation that would directly supply assignment content. I should avoid writing the explanation for them, but I can guide them to produce a 1–2 sentence rationale by pointing to the relevant features of Local Law 97 and asking a focused question. This keeps the response brief, Socratic, and moving toward completion.

exchanges.student: ok then: “Local Law 97 fits the Institutional pathway because it is a city government regulation that sets building emissions limits and creates enforcement mechanisms. It shows how municipal policy can drive climate mitigation through rules and oversight.” does that work, and if yes can u at least tell me one solid example for Physical next?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that works. It’s clear and actually tied to the pathway.

For Physical, think built environment: infrastructure, buildings, transit, flood protection, energy systems. Which NYC climate action sounds most obviously physical to you—retrofitting buildings, coastal protection, or transit improvements?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should affirm the student's reasoning without grading and avoid supplying a full example for the next pathway. To remain Socratic and bite-sized, I’ll note the strengths of the draft and ask a directed question that points them toward the Physical pathway using recognizable NYC categories of action, without composing the entry itself.

exchanges.student: coastal protection prob. tbh if u know the exact project name, just say it and I’ll do the sentence myself.
exchanges.tutor:
Use The Big U, also connected to East Side Coastal Resiliency.

Now do the thinking: what physical thing is being built or changed, and how does that reduce climate risk in NYC?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a specific project name, which is assignment-relevant factual support rather than a full solution. I can provide a concrete example while withholding the explanatory sentence, then immediately prompt the student to articulate why it fits the pathway. This maintains guided discovery and minimal scaffolding.

exchanges.student: got it. for the rest, can u just give me project names only — one each for Spatial, Social, and Economic — no explanations.
exchanges.tutor:
Possible NYC examples to investigate:
Spatial: OneNYC 2050 land-use and resilience planning
Social: Cool Neighborhoods NYC
Economic: NYC Accelerator

Don’t paste those blindly. Pick one and tell me why you think it fits its pathway.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants minimal factual prompts, not full entries. Providing one project/policy name per remaining pathway can be acceptable if I avoid explanations and keep it as a prompt for their own analysis. I should also guard against mismatch by framing them as candidate examples the student must verify and classify.

exchanges.student: alright, I’ll use those. one last thing — can u format the five pathways with the NYC example names in a super simple list so I can keep track while I write?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes — use this as a checklist:

- Institutional — Local Law 97
- Physical — The Big U / East Side Coastal Resiliency
- Spatial — OneNYC 2050 land-use and resilience planning
- Social — Cool Neighborhoods NYC
- Economic — NYC Accelerator

Now write your own 1–2 sentence rationale for each. If you want, send me one and I’ll tell you whether your reasoning fits the pathway.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for an organizational checklist, not written assignment content. I can provide a simple list of pathway-example pairings already discussed, without explanations or prose they could paste as completed analysis. This remains concise and keeps responsibility for the actual writing on the student.

exchanges.student: perfect, thx. I’m gonna draft them now and send one if it sounds off.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Draft all five first, then send me the one you’re least sure about.

Don’t overthink wording—focus on why the example matches the pathway.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should reinforce productive next steps, keep it brief, and maintain the tutor role by inviting the student to share their own draft for feedback rather than offering new content. This supports guided discovery and keeps the student doing the writing.
